data.title: GOAT Industries’ BETSource Strikes BKFC App Deal as Company Boosts Investor Outreach
data.description:
Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

An announcement from GOAT Industries ( (TSE:GOAT) ) is now available.

GOAT Industries’ portfolio company BETSource has entered into a commercial relationship with Bare Knuckle Fighting Championship to integrate its fan engagement and sportsbook attribution technology into the BKFC mobile app, aiming to enhance the user experience, boost in-app interaction and retention, and support BKFC’s monetization efforts as the combat sports promoter rapidly scales its global audience and media distribution. Separately, GOAT has retained 1502655 B.C. Ltd. for a six-month, cash-only investor relations mandate worth up to $400,000 to strengthen its capital markets presence, develop performance metrics for investors, and increase awareness of the company’s strategy and portfolio momentum, signaling a concerted push to raise its profile among market participants.

The most recent analyst rating on (TSE:GOAT) stock is a Hold with a C$0.47 price target. To see the full list of analyst forecasts on GOAT Industries stock, see the TSE:GOAT Stock Forecast page.

Spark’s Take on TSE:GOAT Stock

According to Spark, TipRanks’ AI Analyst, TSE:GOAT is a Neutral.

The score is primarily constrained by very weak financial performance (ongoing losses, negative equity, and negative operating/free cash flow with no reported revenue). Technicals also point to a weak trend (below key moving averages with negative MACD). Positive corporate developments (acquisitions, uplisting, and financing) add some upside potential but carry execution risk and are not yet reflected in profitability.

To see Spark’s full report on TSE:GOAT stock, click here.

More about GOAT Industries

GOAT Industries Ltd. is a Vancouver-based company with a portfolio that includes BETSource, a wholly owned sports media and technology platform focused on fan engagement and sportsbook attribution. Through BETSource, GOAT targets the fast-growing digital sports and combat sports markets by integrating interactive technology into partner apps and ecosystems to deepen user engagement and support monetization strategies.

Average Trading Volume: 123,826

Technical Sentiment Signal: Buy

Current Market Cap: C$16.58M

For an in-depth examination of GOAT stock, go to TipRanks’ Overview page.

data.title: VR Resources Upsizes Brokered Private Placement to $2.75 Million Ahead of Share Consolidation
data.description:
Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

VR Resources ( (TSE:VRR) ) has provided an announcement.

VR Resources Ltd. has further upsized its brokered private placement, led by Centurion One Capital Corp., to raise gross proceeds of up to $2.75 million through the issuance of up to 17,187,500 units priced at $0.16, each comprising one common share and one warrant exercisable at $0.20 for 36 months. The financing is tied to a 10-for-1 share consolidation effective January 15, 2026, which will significantly reduce the number of shares outstanding and may improve the company’s marketability to investors, while the structure of broker commissions and warrants, along with a four‑month hold period and multi-jurisdictional private placement framework, underscores a capital-raising strategy aimed at strengthening VR Resources’ balance sheet and supporting its exploration activities within the constraints of Canadian and U.S. securities regulations.

The most recent analyst rating on (TSE:VRR) stock is a Hold with a C$0.02 price target. To see the full list of analyst forecasts on VR Resources stock, see the TSE:VRR Stock Forecast page.

Spark’s Take on TSE:VRR Stock

According to Spark, TipRanks’ AI Analyst, TSE:VRR is a Neutral.

The score is primarily held back by weak financial performance (no revenue, ongoing losses, and negative free cash flow), only partly mitigated by the zero-debt balance sheet. Technicals are mildly supportive with the price above key moving averages, but momentum indicators are mixed. Valuation is also constrained by a negative P/E and no dividend yield data.

To see Spark’s full report on TSE:VRR stock, click here.

More about VR Resources

VR Resources Ltd. is a Vancouver-based junior mineral exploration company focused on evaluating, exploring and advancing copper, gold and critical metals projects in Nevada, USA, and Ontario, Canada. The company applies modern exploration technologies and in-house greenfields expertise to large-footprint systems in underexplored districts, holds its projects outright, and seeks additional opportunities through staking and acquisitions, leveraging the track record of its board in early-stage exploration, discovery and M&A.

Technical Sentiment Signal: Sell

Current Market Cap: C$2.67M

data.title: Critical Reagent Processing Seeks $236,250 in Private Placement to Advance Argentine Soda Ash Project
data.description:
Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Critical Reagent Processing ( (TSE:CRPC) ) has provided an announcement.

Critical Reagent Processing Corp. has announced a non-brokered private placement of up to 3.5 million common shares at $0.0675 per share, aiming to raise as much as $236,250 in gross proceeds, subject to approval by the Canadian Securities Exchange and a four-month statutory hold period on the issued securities. The funds are earmarked for general working capital, supporting the company’s ongoing advancement of its Laguna Santa Maria soda ash project in Argentina, which is strategically located within a key lithium carbonate production hub and positioned to reduce reliance on soda ash imports, potentially strengthening CRPC’s role in the lithium supply chain and its competitive standing in the Argentine industrial minerals market.

More about Critical Reagent Processing

Critical Reagent Processing Corp. is a Vancouver-based company focused on developing the Mina Laguna Santa Maria project in Salta Province, Argentina, a 500-hectare property hosting a natural deposit of sodium carbonate (soda ash). The project comprises two mining concessions licensed for soda ash production, a key reagent in lithium carbonate manufacturing, and the company now holds 100% of the Ignacio VI concession and 86% of the Santa Maria concession, with documentation filed to obtain the remaining 14%. Positioned in a region of significant lithium carbonate production and development, CRPC aims to leverage its in-country soda ash resource to compete with imported supply into Argentina while it continues to evaluate the property, market conditions, and bulk sample results.

Average Trading Volume: 24,712

Technical Sentiment Signal: Hold

Current Market Cap: C$632.9K

data.title: Lindsay Corp Earnings Call Balances Caution and Confidence
data.description:
Lindsay Corp. ((LNN)) has held its Q1 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Lindsay Corp. Strikes Cautious but Confident Tone Amid Revenue Pressure

Lindsay Corp.’s latest earnings call painted a picture of cautious resilience. Despite a drop in revenue, the company preserved healthy margins, secured a sizable new project in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA), and returned significant cash to shareholders. Management acknowledged persistent macroeconomic pressures and lumpy project timing but expressed guarded optimism about stabilizing demand in North America and solid growth opportunities internationally, underpinned by a strong balance sheet and strategic investments.

Revenue and Profitability: Margins Hold as Sales Slip

Lindsay reported total revenue of $155.8 million, down 6% from $166.3 million a year earlier, reflecting weaker irrigation demand and tough year-over-year comparisons. Operating income also fell 6% to $19.6 million from $20.9 million. However, the company maintained its operating margin at 12.6%, signaling that pricing discipline and cost control are helping protect earnings quality even in a lower-revenue environment. Net earnings dipped slightly to $16.5 million, or $1.54 per diluted share, versus $17.2 million, or $1.57, in the prior-year quarter.

Irrigation Segment: Margin Strength Despite Lower Sales

The irrigation segment, Lindsay’s core business, saw a 9% revenue decline to $133.4 million, pressured by softer demand and international weakness. Yet operating income in irrigation reached $23.0 million, and the segment’s operating margin improved to 17.2% of sales, up from 16.8% a year ago. Management credited pricing actions, cost management, and productivity gains for offsetting volume and mix headwinds, demonstrating that the business can defend profitability even as top-line conditions remain challenging.

Infrastructure: Revenue Growth but Margin Compression

Infrastructure provided a bright spot on growth but showed some profitability pressure. Segment revenue climbed 17% to $22.4 million from $19.2 million, driven mainly by higher sales of road safety products. Operating income rose 9% to $4.5 million from $4.1 million. Still, the infrastructure operating margin slipped to 20.1% from 21.5% as higher operating expenses weighed on results. Management also emphasized that the Road Zipper business will be lumpy, with no large project expected to exit the funnel in fiscal 2026, creating tougher comparisons versus last year’s sizeable shipment.

MENA Project Win Underscores Global Competitiveness

A major highlight of the quarter was Lindsay’s announcement of a roughly $80 million supply agreement for Zimmatic irrigation systems and FieldNET technology in the MENA region, with about $70 million expected to be recognized as revenue this fiscal year. This contract underscores the company’s ability to compete for and secure large-scale global projects in a highly competitive market. While margins on the project are expected to be slightly below segment averages, the win provides meaningful volume and reinforces Lindsay’s position as a technology-enabled irrigation provider on the world stage.

Balance Sheet Strength and Aggressive Share Repurchases

Lindsay emphasized its strong financial position, with total available liquidity of $249.6 million, including $199.6 million in cash and a $50 million undrawn revolver. The company used this balance sheet strength to enhance shareholder returns, repurchasing $30.3 million of stock in the quarter. It also authorized a new share repurchase program of up to $150 million, signaling confidence in the long-term value of the business and offering potential support to earnings per share over time despite modest near-term profit pressure.

Strategic Capital Investments: Building for Future Efficiency

Management highlighted ongoing strategic capital projects aimed at boosting productivity and competitiveness. The company has activated a new large tube mill in Lindsay, Nebraska, which is now near full production, and is progressing on a reengineering of its galvanizing facility. These investments are expected to deliver productivity, safety, and efficiency gains over time. However, incremental depreciation from these projects will likely offset much of the near-term financial benefit until demand strengthens, making these initiatives more of a medium- to long-term margin lever.

EPS Stability Supported by Other Income

Earnings per share slipped only slightly, to $1.54 from $1.57, despite lower operating income and a somewhat higher effective tax rate. Management noted that increased other income, including higher interest income, helped cushion the impact of operating pressure. While not a primary earnings driver, this non-operating support contributed to keeping net income relatively stable in a challenging quarter.

Revenue Declines Led by Irrigation and International Weakness

The overall 6% revenue decline was driven mainly by the irrigation business, where sales fell 9%. International irrigation revenue was particularly weak, dropping about 14.9% from $69.4 million to $59.1 million. North America irrigation fared somewhat better but still declined 4% to $74.3 million. The disparity underscores the sensitivity of Lindsay’s international operations to project timing and macro conditions, while North America appears to be stabilizing at a softer level.

International Markets: Project Timing and Brazil Drag

Management pointed to specific international challenges behind the irrigation downturn. The timing of MENA project revenue recognition led to softer reported results this quarter, even as the underlying project funnel remains robust. In Brazil, elevated interest rates and tight credit are restricting growers’ ability to finance large equipment purchases, leading to deferred investments and weaker demand. The result is more volatility from quarter to quarter and added uncertainty around near-term international performance.

Infrastructure Outlook: Lumpy Road Zipper Activity Weighs on Margins

While infrastructure revenue grew strongly, the segment faces a more complicated outlook. Higher operating expenses compressed margins, and the absence of a large Road Zipper project in fiscal 2026 — compared to a $20 million shipment in the prior-year quarter — sets up difficult comparisons. Current opportunities may offset only about half of this shortfall, with most of the benefit expected in the second half. Investors should expect continued lumpiness in results as project timing plays an outsized role in quarterly performance.

Free Cash Flow and CapEx: Near-Term Pressure, Long-Term Upside

Free cash flow came under pressure as Lindsay increased working capital to support growth and ramped up capital expenditures for its tube mill and galvanizing upgrades. Management acknowledged that elevated capex and higher depreciation will likely offset some of the near-term productivity gains. The company is effectively trading short-term free cash flow for longer-term efficiency and capacity, positioning itself to benefit more fully when end-market demand eventually improves.

Macro Headwinds and Slight Earnings Pressure

The call underscored ongoing macro headwinds: trade uncertainty, low commodity prices, high input costs, and constrained grower credit — particularly in Brazil — are all dampening farmer profitability and delaying major capital spending on irrigation equipment. These pressures contributed to lower operating income and a slightly higher effective tax rate, which together nudged net earnings down year over year despite help from higher other income. Management characterized the environment as challenging but manageable, with structural demand drivers intact over the longer term.

Forward-Looking Guidance: Soft Near Term, Select Growth Drivers

Looking ahead, Lindsay’s guidance remained cautious. The company expects North America irrigation demand to stay “soft,” and is planning for a flat-to-down full-year run rate relative to current levels, after a 4% year-over-year decline in Q1. Overall irrigation revenue fell 9% in the quarter, and management does not anticipate a quick turnaround given persistent macro and credit pressures, notably in Brazil. That said, the roughly $80 million MENA irrigation project — with about $70 million to be recognized this fiscal year — and a healthy pipeline of international projects should provide a meaningful revenue tailwind, even if margins on the project are modestly below segment averages. Infrastructure is expected to grow but face a drag from the absence of a large Road Zipper project in fiscal 2026, with new opportunities likely offsetting only part of last year’s $20 million comparable shipment. Management reiterated that liquidity remains strong at $249.6 million, share repurchases will continue to be an important capital allocation tool, and margin improvements from recent capex are more likely to materialize after demand recovers, given higher near-term depreciation.

In summary, Lindsay’s earnings call reflected a company navigating a tough demand backdrop while preserving profitability, investing for the future, and rewarding shareholders. Revenue and earnings are under modest pressure, especially in international irrigation, but strong margins, a major MENA win, robust liquidity, and disciplined capital deployment provide a solid foundation. For investors, the story is one of short-term caution balanced by long-term opportunity, with the timing of a demand recovery likely to be the key driver of future upside.

data.title: Roots Corp Earnings Call Signals Growth With Caution
data.description:
Roots Corp ((TSE:ROOT)) has held its Q3 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Roots Corp’s latest earnings call painted a picture of a business regaining momentum, with healthy top-line growth, fatter margins, and improving leverage offset by higher spending and some seasonal risks. Management emphasized that stronger sales, better profitability and clear brand traction justify elevated marketing and inventory levels ahead of the key holiday quarter, while cautioning that execution in Q4 will be crucial amid a discount-heavy retail backdrop.

Revenue Growth: Fifth Straight Quarter of Top-Line Gains

Roots delivered Q3 revenue of $71.5 million, up 6.8% from $66.9 million a year earlier, marking its fifth consecutive quarter of growth. The performance underscores renewed demand momentum across the business as the company benefits from improved product assortments, targeted marketing and stronger customer engagement. For investors, the consistency of top-line gains is notable in a retail environment where many brands are struggling to maintain growth.

Direct-to-Consumer Strength and Comparable Store Sales

Direct-to-consumer remains the core engine of the business, with DTC revenue rising 4.8% to $56.8 million from $54.2 million last year. Comparable store sales climbed 6.3% year-over-year and 12.1% on a two-year basis, fueled by higher traffic, better conversion and new customer acquisition. These metrics suggest that Roots’ stores and online platforms are resonating with shoppers, and that recent merchandising and marketing initiatives are translating into tangible sales productivity rather than just price-driven volume.

Partner and Wholesale Channels Deliver Double-Digit Growth

Beyond its own stores and e-commerce, Roots saw strong momentum in partner and wholesale channels. Partners and other sales jumped 15.3% to $14.6 million from $12.7 million, helped by earlier orders from a Taiwanese wholesale partner and improved B2B activity. This diversification helps smooth revenue and extends the brand’s reach internationally, providing an additional growth lever alongside the DTC business.

Gross Margin Expansion Signals Pricing and Product Discipline

Profitability at the gross margin level improved meaningfully. Total gross margin rose to 60.8%, up 80 basis points year-over-year, while DTC gross margin advanced 140 basis points to 65.4%. Management attributed the gains to reduced markdowns, a stronger product mix and sourcing and freight efficiencies. In a heavily promotional retail climate, Roots’ ability to expand margins while growing sales indicates greater pricing power and more disciplined inventory management.

Profitability and Adjusted EBITDA Trends Point to Operating Leverage

Adjusted EBITDA edged up 5.3% to $7.5 million from $7.1 million a year ago, reflecting improving operational efficiency. Management noted that excluding the impact of DSU/share-based compensation revaluation, adjusted EBITDA would have increased roughly 7.3%–7.4% year-over-year, underscoring underlying operating leverage. While not explosive, this steady improvement shows that Roots is turning incremental revenue into incremental profit even as it leans into higher marketing spend.

Balance Sheet and Leverage Show Gradual De-Risking

Roots continued to chip away at its debt, with net debt falling 5.9% to $44.1 million from $46.9 million last year. Net leverage now stands at roughly 1.9 times trailing 12-month adjusted EBITDA. For investors, this signals a more resilient balance sheet and greater financial flexibility, particularly important as the company navigates seasonal working capital swings and invests in brand-building initiatives.

Operational and Brand Momentum Underpins Strategy

Management highlighted a series of wins that support the growth narrative beyond the raw numbers. Improved style productivity and strong reception for new collections—such as the Roam travel capsule and the Cloud line—suggest that product innovation is connecting with consumers. New store activations in Vancouver and Toronto, a presence at the University of Toronto, and a high-profile campaign featuring Seth Rogen all helped drive early holiday engagement. These efforts reinforce Roots’ positioning as a lifestyle brand, not just a traditional apparel retailer.

Working Capital and Cash Flow: Seasonal Outflow, but Improving Trend

Free cash flow remained negative in Q3, with a $4.6 million outflow, yet this marked an improvement from a $6.0 million outflow in the prior year. The company attributed the seasonal cash use to higher inventory and working capital ahead of peak holiday selling, partially offset by sales growth and better working capital management. While investors will want to see positive cash generation over the full year, the better year-on-year trend suggests Roots is managing its seasonal cash demands more efficiently.

Rising SG&A: Investing Behind Growth and Brand Equity

Selling, general and administrative expenses climbed 10.6% to $38.2 million from $34.5 million, outpacing revenue growth. This uptick reflects higher marketing investments—including an expected additional $2–$3 million in Q4—along with increased personnel costs and variable selling expenses tied to stronger sales. Management framed these costs as deliberate investments to support both near-term growth and longer-term brand equity, a trade-off investors will watch closely for payback in future quarters.

Net Income Slightly Lower on Share-Based Compensation Revaluation

Reported net income slipped 4.5% to $2.3 million, compared with $2.4 million a year ago, with earnings per share steady at $0.06 in both periods. The decline was driven largely by revaluation of DSU/share-based compensation linked to share price appreciation—an accounting impact rather than a core operational issue. Excluding this factor, management said net income would have improved about 1.5%, suggesting underlying profitability is edging higher despite heavier spending.

Inventory Buildup Adds Seasonal Risk

Inventory ended the quarter at $66.6 million, up 10.3% from $60.4 million a year earlier, with foreign exchange also playing a role. The build reflects planned stock and in-transit goods ahead of the holiday season, but it increases exposure if consumer demand softens or promotional intensity spikes. While higher inventory can support sales growth and reduce stockouts, it also raises the stakes for Q4 performance and the company’s ability to avoid excessive markdowns.

Discounting Pressures Persist Across the Market

Management noted that discounting remained prevalent in the broader market, including both early and post-Black Friday promotions. Such conditions can pressure margins and potentially undercut full-price selling, even as Roots itself has made progress reducing markdowns. The company’s margin expansion this quarter suggests it is managing the environment better than many peers, but persistent discounting remains a structural headwind to watch.

Tariff and U.S. E-commerce Headwinds

Roots also pointed to incremental external pressures from trade and cross-border changes. The removal of U.S. duty-free de minimis thresholds added roughly $0.3 million in costs, and new cross-border duty rules have created modest headwinds for U.S. e-commerce sales. While these numbers are manageable today, they limit some upside from the U.S. DTC opportunity and add another layer of complexity to pricing and profitability decisions.

Seasonal Cash Outflow Still a Feature of the Model

Despite the improvement in free cash flow, Q3 still saw a $4.6 million cash outflow as Roots built inventory and working capital for the holidays. This underscores the seasonal nature of the business and the cash requirements associated with preparing for peak periods. Investors should expect similar patterns in future years, with the focus shifting to how efficiently the company converts that seasonal investment into cash during and after the holiday quarter.

Guidance and Outlook: Investing Heavily Ahead of Holiday and Beyond

Looking ahead, management reiterated its strategy of investing behind the brand while relying on recent performance to justify the spend. Roots plans to deploy an additional $2–$3 million of marketing in Q4, continuing to test paid media, AI-driven discovery and site personalization in order to better convert traffic into sales. The company pointed to its Q3 scorecard—6.8% revenue growth, 4.8% DTC growth, 15.3% partner and other growth, gross margin expansion, and adjusted EBITDA improvement—as evidence that the strategy is working. Net debt is down to $44.1 million with net leverage around 1.9x, and the company also repurchased 415,000 shares for $1.3 million, signaling confidence in its equity. Management remains focused on maximizing holiday conversion and using real-time data to refine next year’s marketing mix, while keeping a close eye on inventory and promotional dynamics.

Roots’ earnings call ultimately balanced optimism with realism: the company is growing, margins are improving and the brand is gaining traction, but these gains come alongside higher spending, tariff pressures and seasonal risks. For investors, the key watchpoints heading into Q4 will be the conversion of heavy holiday traffic into profitable sales and the company’s ability to manage elevated inventory without sacrificing margin. If Roots continues to execute as it has over the past five quarters, the current investment phase could set up a stronger, more profitable growth trajectory in the year ahead.

data.title: Highlander Silver and Bear Creek Streamline Acquisition Agreement by Dropping Highlander Shareholder Vote
data.description:

Bear Creek Mining ( (TSE:BCM) ) just unveiled an update.

Highlander Silver Corp. and Bear Creek Mining Corporation have amended and restated their previously announced arrangement agreement under which Highlander Silver will acquire all Bear Creek shares it does not already own by way of a statutory plan of arrangement. Following the exercise of a sufficient number of Highlander Silver share purchase warrants, the deal structure has been revised to remove the need for a Highlander Silver shareholder vote under Toronto Stock Exchange rules, streamlining the transaction timeline while maintaining all other key terms, with Bear Creek securityholders now set to vote on the arrangement by February 19, 2026, a step that advances consolidation of these Latin American precious metals assets under Highlander’s control.

While technical indicators show some positive momentum, the negative valuation metrics further weigh down the score. Strategic improvements are essential for enhancing financial health and investor confidence.

More about Bear Creek Mining

Bear Creek Mining Corporation is a precious metals producer with operations and development assets in Latin America, including the cash-generating Mercedes gold mine in Sonora, Mexico, and the Corani Project in Puno, Peru, which is described as one of the largest fully permitted silver deposits globally with substantial reserves, low projected operating costs and strong community support.

data.title: Matador Technologies Grants RSUs to Bolster Bitcoin-First Growth Strategy
data.description:
Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

An update from Matador Technologies Inc. ( (TSE:MATA) ) is now available.

Matador Technologies Inc. has granted 631,818 restricted share units to certain officers and consultants under its Omnibus Equity Incentive Plan, with the awards vesting over 12 months starting January 8, 2026. The equity grants are designed to retain and incentivize the management team as Matador advances its Bitcoin-first strategy and capital markets objectives, reinforcing executive alignment with shareholders at a time when the company is expanding its role in Bitcoin-focused financial infrastructure and pursuing international investments such as its proposed stake in India’s HODL Systems.

More about Matador Technologies Inc.

Matador Technologies Inc. is a publicly traded Bitcoin ecosystem company that holds Bitcoin as its primary treasury asset and develops products to enhance the Bitcoin network. Its strategy combines strategic Bitcoin accumulation, Bitcoin-native product development, and participation in digital asset infrastructure, with a focus on long-term shareholder value and capital efficiency. The company has also moved to expand its global footprint through a proposed investment in India-based digital asset treasury firm HODL Systems, aiming to secure up to a 24% stake and strengthen its position as a leading Bitcoin treasury player.
